aliases: Turkish spinach and cheese boat bread

english: Turkish spinach and cheese boat bread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Turkish pide. FOODON_03315671 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX (Flat bread) is the closest parent. No specific pide code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Turkish pide or stuffed flatbread at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole spinach pide as typically served at a Turkish or Middle Eastern restaurant, weighing approximately 350 g, constitutes a single serving.

nutrient_source.title: Spinach and cheese pide — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Bread, pita, white, enriched (FDC ID 325871) and Spinach, raw (FDC ID 168462) and Cheese, feta (FDC ID 173420)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for spinach pide exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: white flatbread dough (~55% by weight), spinach filling (~25%), and feta/white cheese (~20%). Weighted average calculated per 100 g of finished baked product. Energy cross-checked against standard Turkish pide nutritional references in peer-reviewed literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of flatbreads and leavened wheat breads — analogous estimate based on pita bread GI (57) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spinach pide. GI of 57 is estimated from white pita bread (GI 57, University of Sydney GI Database), which is the closest structurally analogous leavened wheat flatbread. The spinach and cheese filling contributes negligible additional glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (57 × 35.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 71.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (420 mg per 100 g; approximately 1470 mg per full serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit spinach pide to half a serving or less, as a full 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and high glycemic load per full serving (GL ~71)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume spinach pide in small portions (100–150 g) to limit glycemic load, pairing it with additional non-starchy vegetables or a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese (3.1 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, spinach pide should be consumed occasionally and in reduced portions, given its combined saturated fat and sodium load; choosing lower-sodium cheese variants and smaller portions can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spinach pide (Turkish: Ispanaklı Pide) is a traditional Turkish baked flatbread shaped like an elongated boat or canoe, filled with a mixture of fresh or wilted spinach, white cheese (typically beyaz peynir or feta), onion, and egg, then baked in a wood-fired or conventional oven until the crust is golden and slightly crisp. The dough is a leavened wheat dough similar to pizza dough, yielding a chewy, airy crumb with a lightly charred exterior. The filling is savoury, mildly tangy from the cheese, and earthy from the spinach. Pide is a staple of Turkish cuisine and is widely served across the Middle East and in Turkish diaspora restaurants globally, including in Singapore. Nutritionally, spinach pide provides moderate energy (~242 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from cheese and egg (~8.5 g per 100 g), and useful micronutrients including calcium, iron, folate, and vitamins A and C from the spinach component. Its medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per full serving make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) is clinically relevant for hypertension management. Spinach pide is vegetarian and halal-suitable, making it broadly accessible across Singapore's diverse population.
common_user_queries: How many calories in spinach pide?
Is spinach pide good for diabetics?
What is Turkish flatbread with spinach called?
How much sodium is in pide?
What is the GI of pide?
What cheese is used in spinach pide?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for spinach pide exists in any primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for white flatbread, spinach, and feta cheese using a weighted composition model. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (white pita bread, GI 57) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central (white flatbread dough ~55%, spinach ~25%, feta/white cheese ~20% by weight of finished product). Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources (flatbread, spinach, feta) used for estimation, and is expected to be negligible in this product. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from white pita bread (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct pide GI measurement was found in the literature. GL per serving = (57 × 35.8 × 350) / 10000 = 71.3. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Vegetables' (input) to 'Grains and staples' as the primary caloric and structural component is the wheat dough. The input food_name 'Pide Spinach' has been normalised to the canonical English form 'Spinach Pide'. Served at Sahara Bar and Restaurant, 58 Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source). Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy Middle Eastern cuisine served in Singapore context; Turkish romanized form also provided. All aliases_by_language entries are confirmed present in the flat aliases list.
